worth_it_explanation: First-party data is the input that keeps Meta Ads measurable as cookies and device identifiers fade. The checks here read settings and scores that Meta already exposes, so the work is inspection rather than rebuilding anything. A Customer List uploaded without a documented lawful basis is a compliance exposure that the account owner carries. A Limited Data Use flag left unset in a regulated state changes how Meta may process the data. A low Event Match Quality (EMQ) score means the Conversions API is sending events Meta cannot match well, so attribution and bidding both work from thinner data than the traffic would support. Each finding points to a specific setting or score to confirm, and the fixes are configuration and data-hygiene changes rather than new builds.
intro: First-party data is the information an advertiser collects directly from its own customers, such as an email address entered at checkout or a phone number given on a form. As browsers restrict third-party cookies and Apple's App Tracking Transparency (ATT) limits device-level signals, that directly collected data is what Meta uses to match conversions and build audiences. These checks confirm that Customer Lists are uploaded with a documented lawful basis, that the data-sharing and Limited Data Use controls are set for the regions an advertiser serves, and that the Conversions API is sending enough quality identifiers to match events well.

audit_checks.title: Customer Lists are uploaded without a documented lawful basis for the data
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Accepting the Customer List Custom Audiences terms is a representation that the advertiser has the rights and consent to use the data for Meta advertising. If that basis was never documented, the account owner carries a compliance exposure that surfaces in a regulator inquiry or a deletion request the advertiser cannot honor, not in the campaign metrics.

educate.base:
A Customer List Custom Audience is built by uploading customer contact data, such as emails or phone numbers, which Meta hashes and matches against accounts to define an audience for targeting. Before an upload runs, the advertiser accepts the Customer List Custom Audiences terms, which state that the advertiser has the necessary rights and a lawful basis to use that data and will remove anyone who opts out.

The audit question is whether that representation is backed by a record. The consent or other lawful basis collected at the point a customer gave their email, the data-retention practice behind the list, and a way to remove an individual who later opts out are the substance behind the acceptance click. A list assembled from purchases years ago, scraped from a third party, or carried over from a tool the customer never agreed to is the case the terms are written to exclude.

A few signs suggest a Customer List may lack a documented basis:
  • Source: the list was exported from another platform or bought, with no record of how the contacts were collected
  • Consent: the signup or checkout that captured the email did not mention advertising use or audience sharing
  • Opt-out: there is no process to remove a contact from the uploaded audience when they unsubscribe or request deletion
You may want to confirm the lawful basis and the opt-out process are written down before treating an uploaded list as compliant. The acceptance click records that the advertiser agreed to the terms, not that the data behind it meets them.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, the Customer List is often a purchaser export from the commerce platform or email service, so the consent that matters is what the checkout and account signup actually told the customer. You may want to confirm the checkout's privacy language covers advertising use, and that an unsubscribe or deletion in the store removes the contact from the uploaded audience rather than leaving a stale copy in Meta.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, the Customer List is usually built from form fills and a customer relationship management (CRM) export, where leads may have agreed only to be contacted about an inquiry. You may want to confirm the form's consent language covers advertising use specifically since consent to a sales follow-up is not the same as consent to upload the contact to Meta for audience targeting.

learn_more.source_title: Prepare your data for a customer list custom audience

investigate.intro: Review the Customer List audiences in the account, then trace each one back to the data source and the consent collected where the contacts were captured.

meta_business_suite.label: Meta Business Suite
steps: Open 'Business Settings', then 'Audiences', or open the Audiences section of Ads Manager.
Filter to Customer List audiences and list each one with its size and the date it was last updated.
For each Customer List, identify the data source it was built from, for example a CRM export, an email platform, or a purchased file.
For each source, locate the consent or other lawful basis recorded at the point the contacts were collected, and confirm there is a process to remove a contact who opts out.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per Customer List audience, the data source, whether a documented lawful basis exists for advertising use, and whether an opt-out removal process exists. An audience with a recorded source, a documented basis covering advertising use, and a working opt-out path passes. An audience built from an unknown or purchased source, or with no documented basis, or with no opt-out path, is a fail recorded with the audience name and the gap.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the source, the documented lawful basis, and the opt-out process for each Customer List audience above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more Customer List audiences on {{site_name}} were uploaded without a documented lawful basis for using the data in Meta advertising. Accepting the Customer List Custom Audiences terms is a representation that {{site_name}} has the rights and consent to use the contacts and can remove anyone who opts out, and that representation needs a record behind it. {{voice_recommend}} documenting the source and the consent collected for each Customer List, confirming the signup or checkout language covers advertising use, and putting a process in place to remove a contact from the audience when they unsubscribe or request deletion. Where a list's basis cannot be established, {{voice_recommend}} pausing its use until the source is verified.

audit_checks.title: The Limited Data Use flag is not set for users in regulated US states
audit_checks.impact_rationale: The Limited Data Use flag controls whether Meta acts as a service provider for data about people in certain US states. Left unset where it is required, the advertiser may be sharing data in a way its own privacy commitments do not cover. The exposure is a compliance one rather than a metric, and enabling it does reduce retargeting reach for the affected users.

educate.base:
Limited Data Use (LDU) is a Meta flag that tells Meta to act as a service provider for the data an advertiser sends about people in specific US states, rather than using it for its own broad ad purposes. It exists so an advertiser can meet US state privacy laws, such as California's, when sending events through the Meta Pixel and the Conversions API. For people in covered states, Meta processes the data for analytics and the advertiser's own measurement but not for cross-context behavioral advertising.

The flag is set per event by passing data-processing-options fields. An advertiser can let Meta detect the user's state, or pass the country and state directly. If those fields are absent on events from people in a state where the advertiser has committed to honoring opt-outs, the data flows under Meta's default processing, which may exceed what the advertiser's privacy notice and any signaled opt-out allow.

A few signs suggest LDU is not set where it should be:
  • Coverage: the consent management platform or tag setup does not pass data-processing-options for users in regulated states
  • Signal: an opt-out signal, such as Global Privacy Control, is honored on the site but not reflected in what reaches Meta
  • Surfaces: the Pixel passes the flag but server events through the Conversions API do not, or the reverse
You may want to confirm the flag is set consistently across both the Pixel and the Conversions API. Enabling it reduces retargeting and measurement for the covered users, so the setting is a deliberate compliance choice rather than a default to leave on everywhere.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store serving customers across US states, the same checkout event can need different handling by state, so the Limited Data Use flag has to be set from a signal the site already has about the shopper's region. You may want to confirm the data-processing-options fields are applied at the purchase event for customers in covered states, on both the browser Pixel and any server-side Conversions API events for the same purchase.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, the regulated event is usually a form submission, and the visitor's state may not be known until they enter it. You may want to confirm the tag setup either lets Meta detect the state or reads the state from the form before the lead event fires, so the Limited Data Use treatment is applied rather than missed because the region was resolved too late.

learn_more.source_title: Data Processing Options for US Users (Meta Pixel)

investigate.intro: Confirm whether the Limited Data Use flag is set for users in regulated states, and check that the setting is applied consistently across the Pixel and the Conversions API.

browser_devtools.label: Browser DevTools
steps: Open the site in a browser, open DevTools, and watch the Network tab while triggering a tracked event such as a purchase or a lead.
Find the Pixel request to Meta and check whether it carries the data-processing-options fields, for example 'dpo', 'dpoco', and 'dpost'.
Repeat from a context that represents a regulated state, or with any opt-out signal the site honors active, and confirm the flag changes as expected.

meta_events_manager.label: Meta Events Manager
steps: Open the dataset in Events Manager and review the events received from both the Pixel and the Conversions API.
Check whether server events for users in covered states carry the same data-processing-options treatment as the browser events since the two surfaces are configured separately.
Note any case where one surface applies the flag and the other does not for the same conversion.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for events from regulated states, whether data-processing-options are passed on the Pixel and on the Conversions API, and whether the treatment matches any opt-out signal the site honors. Consistent application across both surfaces for covered users passes. The flag missing on either surface, or not reflecting an honored opt-out, is a fail recorded with the surface and the state coverage at fault.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the Limited Data Use status on the Pixel and the Conversions API for events from regulated states above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the Limited Data Use flag is not set for users in regulated US states on {{site_name}}, so data about those people reaches Meta under default processing rather than the service-provider terms the flag applies. Where {{site_name}} has committed to honoring opt-outs or operates under a state privacy law, that gap can put the data sharing outside what the privacy notice allows. {{voice_recommend}} configuring the data-processing-options fields for users in covered states, applying the same treatment on both the Pixel and the Conversions API, and confirming the flag reflects any opt-out signal the site honors. {{voice_recommend}} treating the reduced retargeting reach this brings as the expected cost of the compliance setting.

audit_checks.title: Event Match Quality is weak because the Conversions API sends sparse identifiers
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Event Match Quality measures how well Meta can match server events to accounts. A weak score means fewer events match, so attribution undercounts conversions and the bidding that learns from them works from thinner data. The setup looks complete because events arrive, which is why the weak match goes unnoticed.

educate.base:
Event Match Quality (EMQ) is a score Meta assigns to a dataset's events, based on how many customer-information parameters the Conversions API sends, how clean those parameters are, and what share of events match to a Meta account. The Conversions API is Meta's server-to-server connection for sending events, and it can carry identifiers a browser pixel cannot, such as a hashed email, a hashed phone number, a name, and location fields. The more accepted parameters arrive in a usable form, the higher the share of events Meta can match.

A dataset can receive every event and still score poorly. Sending only an event name and an Internet Protocol (IP) address, hashing a field incorrectly, or passing an empty value where an email was expected all lower the match rate. The events are recorded, so the connection looks healthy, while attribution runs on a fraction of the matches the same traffic could support.

The first-party angle is the input. A strong score depends on the advertiser actually collecting and passing customer identifiers it has a basis to use. An account that captures an email at checkout but never passes it to the Conversions API is leaving match quality on the table.

A few signs point to weak match quality from sparse identifiers:
  • Parameters: the Conversions API sends only an event name and an IP address, with no hashed email or phone number
  • Format: a field is passed unhashed where hashing is expected, or normalized inconsistently before hashing
  • Coverage: identifiers are present on some events, such as purchases, but missing on others the advertiser optimizes toward
You may want to read the match quality score per event and trace a low one back to the parameters the server actually sends. The fix is in what the integration passes, not in turning the connection on.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, the purchase event already has rich customer data attached, so a weak Event Match Quality score usually means the integration is not forwarding the email, phone, and address fields the order contains. You may want to confirm the server event for a purchase passes the hashed email and phone alongside the order since those are the parameters that lift the match rate most on commerce traffic.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, the strongest identifier is the email or phone captured in the form, so a weak score often means the lead event reaches Meta with little more than an IP address. You may want to confirm the form's email and phone are normalized, hashed, and passed on the lead event through the Conversions API, rather than relying on the browser signals alone.

learn_more.source_title: Dataset Quality API for the Conversions API

investigate.intro: Read the Event Match Quality score for the dataset's key events, then trace a weak score back to the customer-information parameters the Conversions API sends.

meta_events_manager.label: Meta Events Manager
steps: Open the dataset in Events Manager and select a key event, such as Purchase or Lead.
Read the Event Match Quality score for that event and note which customer-information parameters Meta reports as received.
Compare the parameters received against what the event could carry, for example a hashed email and phone the page or form has, and note any that are missing or flagged as poorly formatted.
Repeat for each event the account optimizes toward since match quality is scored per event rather than per dataset.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per key event, the Event Match Quality score and the customer-information parameters the Conversions API sends. An event with a strong score and the identifiers the advertiser has a basis to pass passes. A weak score traced to missing, unhashed, or empty identifiers is a fail recorded with the event name and the parameters at fault.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the Event Match Quality score and the parameters sent for each key event above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that Event Match Quality on {{site_name}} is weak because the Conversions API sends sparse customer identifiers. The events arrive, so the connection reads as healthy, but Meta matches only a fraction of them to accounts, which means attribution undercounts conversions and the bidding that learns from them works from thinner data than the traffic supports. {{voice_recommend}} forwarding the hashed identifiers the advertiser already collects and has a basis to use, such as email and phone, on the key events, normalizing and hashing each field as Meta expects, and confirming identifiers are present on every event the account optimizes toward. After the change, recheck the Event Match Quality score per event in Events Manager.
